1:
The chapter opens with a bare date — "in the eighteenth year of king Jeroboam began Abijah to reign over Judah" — but the witnesses hear in it the announcement of a whole history that the book of Kings had left untold. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw the contrast sharply: in Kings it is merely remarked in general that hostility persisted between the two houses, and that there was war between Abijah and Jeroboam, but not one of Abijah's enterprises is recounted, and only his attitude toward the Lord is characterized. Here, by contrast, we are given a vivid and circumstantial narrative of the commencement, course, and results of a great war, in which Abijah, with the help of the LORD, inflicted a crushing defeat on the huge army of Israel and conquered several of its cities. The regnal notice itself, they observe, agrees with the parallel at 1 Kings 15:1.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry weighs the theological warrant for the war. God gave Abijah leave to engage Jeroboam and owned him in the conflict, though he had forbidden his father Rehoboam to fight the ten tribes back at 2 Chronicles 11:4. The difference, Henry reasons, lay in the aims: Rehoboam had sought the utter reduction of the ten tribes, contrary to the counsel of God, whereas Abijah seems to have intended no more than to chastise an aggressor. Jeroboam, he suggests, was probably now the aggressor, perhaps hoping on this young king's accession to seize the crown of Judah itself; against such pretensions it was brave in Abijah to take up arms, and God stood by him. When Rehoboam had earlier attempted the ten tribes Jeroboam was still on his good behaviour and untried; but now that he had shown what manner of man he was, setting up the calves and casting off the priests, Abijah is allowed to correct him.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays out the shape of the chapter in advance — the reign, the war, the speech from Mount Zemaraim, the ambush, the cry to the Lord, the great slaughter, the recovery of cities, and the smiting of Jeroboam. The reformed John Gill contents himself with fixing the date, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the philological caution that the opening "Now" is not in the Hebrew and that the verse stands nearly identical with its parallel in Kings. Together the witnesses set the stage: a chronological notice that is really the doorway into the one campaign the Chronicler thought worth telling at length, because in it the question of the whole book — who truly holds the kingdom of the LORD — was about to be tried by battle.
2:
The verse gives the length of the reign and the name of the queen mother, and it is the mother's name that draws the witnesses into a small textual tangle. "He reigned three years in Jerusalem," the text says, "and his mother's name was Michaiah the daughter of Uriel of Gibeah" — yet the parallel at 1 Kings 15:2 calls her Maachah the daughter of Abishalom, and the Chronicler himself had already named her Maachah the daughter of Absalom at 2 Chronicles 11:20. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott judges the matter beyond doubt: "Michaiah," elsewhere a man's name, is a corruption of Maachah, a reading confirmed by the Septuagint, the Syriac, and the Arabic. Maachah, he explains, was granddaughter to Absalom, being a daughter of Tamar, Absalom's only daughter named at 2 Samuel 14:27; and Uriel of Gibeah, otherwise unknown, must therefore have been the husband of Tamar. So the Chronicler, on Ellicott's reading, has actually preserved a genuine detail that Kings omits — the queen mother's own father — even while a copyist's slip has disguised her personal name as a man's.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch reach the same conclusion by another road, taking Michaiah to be a mere orthographical error for Maachah rather than a genuine second name given for some unknown reason. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, less sure of any emendation, harmonizes the two records by supposing that Maachah was a granddaughter of Absalom through his daughter Tamar, her immediate father being this Uriel — so that she is called daughter of Absalom in one place by descent and daughter of Uriel in another by birth. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke simply refers the reader back to his note at 2 Chronicles 11:20, and the reformed John Gill passes the question by.

On the closing clause the witnesses turn from genealogy to the sober fact that frames the chapter: "and there was war between Abijah and Jeroboam." Ellicott notes that the Chronicler has taken this from 1 Kings 15:6, where war is said to have prevailed "all the days of his life," but has deliberately omitted that concluding phrase — and then proceeded to give a striking account of a single campaign of which Kings says not a word. Keil and Delitzsch likewise read the notice as the seed from which the whole narrative grows, "war arose, broke out," the formula that launches the account of Abijah's victory. The witnesses thus converge: a corrupted name is quietly corrected, a genealogy through Tamar recovered, and the reader turned toward the war that the rest of the chapter will describe.
3:
"Abijah set the battle in array with an army of valiant men of war, even four hundred thousand chosen men" — and Jeroboam met him with eight hundred thousand — and at once the witnesses divide over whether these staggering figures can be trusted. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke finds them scarcely credible and suspects a cipher too many has crept into each: he would read forty thousand, eighty thousand, and (at v.17) fifty thousand slain. He marshals a long array of evidence for the smaller numbers — the Syriac and Arabic, dozens of early printed Vulgates, and many manuscripts of his own collection that read "forty thousand" and "eighty thousand" — concluding that corruptions in the numbers of these historical books are frequent, and that the lesser figures appear the more correct.

The grammatical-historical witnesses defend the received text. Keil and Delitzsch observe that Jeroboam's eight hundred thousand is exactly the figure Joab returned for Israel in David's census, while Abijah's four hundred thousand falls only a hundred thousand short of Judah's muster there, as recorded at 2 Samuel 24:9. Charles Ellicott presses the same correspondence: in the census of 1 Chronicles 21:5 Judah mustered four hundred and seventy thousand and Israel eleven hundred thousand, and the still closer agreement with the totals of 2 Samuel 24:9 — Israel eight hundred thousand, Judah five hundred thousand — makes it improbable, he argues, that the figures have been falsified in transmission. Ellicott adds that "set the battle in array" means simply "began the battle," with Jeroboam drawing up his host over against Abijah, and Keil and Delitzsch that the underlying Hebrew idiom is "to bind on war," that is, to open the campaign — the very exactness of the numbers, they hold, telling against the theory of a scribe's inflation rather than for it.

The reformed John Gill notes only that Abijah gathered such an army of select men, led them into the enemy's country, and set them in order of battle. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lets the disproportion carry his lesson: Jeroboam's army was double Abijah's, for he had ten tribes to draw from and Abijah but two; both hosts were of mighty, chosen, and valiant men, yet the inferior number proved victorious, "for the battle is not always to the strong, nor the cause to the majority." So the verse leaves two questions side by side — whether the numbers are literal or swollen in copying, a matter on which Clarke and the grammatical-historical school genuinely differ — and the deeper point on which none of them divides, that a host twice the size would be broken by the smaller army that had God on its side.
4:
Abijah does not open with the sword but with a speech, taking his stand "upon Mount Zemaraim, which is in Mount Ephraim," and the witnesses attend both to the geography and to the strategy of a king who reasons before he fights. The reformed John Gill supposes the mountain took its name from a city of Benjamin called Zemaraim, lying within the borders of Ephraim; Abijah stood there that he might be the better heard by the armies in the valley, and, Gill infers, had very probably requested and been granted a parley, since otherwise he would not have been safe in so exposed a position.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that this mountain is met with only here, though a city of the name appears at Joshua 18:22; following Robinson they place Mount Zemaraim to the east of Bethel, near the border of the two kingdoms, to which the hill country of Ephraim also extended. Charles Ellicott agrees that the site is otherwise unknown and probably lay a little south of Bethel on the northern frontier of Judah. He hears in the very construction the Chronicler's own hand — the Hebrew reads literally "from upon Mount Zemaraim" — and draws a telling parallel: Abijah addressed his foes from the mountain as Jotham had addressed the men of Shechem from the top of Gerizim in the days of the judges, at Judges 9:7. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, citing the Targum and Jarchi, likewise takes it for a height on the confines of Ephraim, and is careful to distinguish it from Shomeron, the hill on which Samaria was later built.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the scene into a maxim. It is good, he says, to try reason before we use force; if the point may be gained by dint of argument, better so than by dint of sword, and we must never fly to violent methods till all the arts of persuasion have been tried in vain — for war must be the last resort of kings. Abijah had pressed into the very heart of the enemy's country, and from this hill he could be heard by Jeroboam and his chief officers, with whom he evidently sought a treaty; "how forcible are right words!" The witnesses thus frame the verse as the pause before battle in which a king of Judah, standing on a contested mountain, offers his adversaries an argument before he offers them a war — a courtesy that Jeroboam, as the sequel will show, will not return.
5:
The speech proper begins with a challenge to memory — "Ought ye not to know that the LORD God of Israel gave the kingdom over Israel to David for ever, even to him and to his sons by a covenant of salt?" — and the witnesses gather around that ancient and arresting phrase. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the opening as a construction characteristic of the Chronicler, "is it not to you to know?", by which Abijah sets his own moral position over against his adversaries': their very existence as a separate kingdom is an act of rebellion against Jehovah, they have abolished the only legitimate worship, and he and his people alone have kept the orthodox ritual. The "covenant of salt," he explains, means a firm and unalterable compact; salt was indispensable at formal meals for ratifying friendship and alliance, and so accompanied the sacrifices as a renewal of the covenant between man and God, as at Leviticus 2:13 and Numbers 18:19 — and the very antiquity of the phrase, he adds, bears on the authenticity of the speech.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the same sense more briefly: the phrase describes an irrevocable covenant, after the fashion of salt that never loses its savour. The wesleyan-arminian witnesses press the image home. Adam Clarke cites the Targum's beautiful gloss — "as the waters of the sea never grow sweet, neither shall the dominion depart from the house of David" — and points back to his note at Numbers 18:19; John Wesley reduces it to its plain force, "a perpetual covenant."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the argument Abijah is building. The king claims a divine right: God had given the kingdom to David and his sons for ever, not by the common providence with which he ordinarily disposes of kingdoms, but by a covenant of salt, a lasting covenant made by sacrifice, which was always salted — so Henry follows bishop Patrick. All Israel, Henry notes, had owned that David was a king of God's making and that the crown was entailed upon his family; and though Jeroboam had indeed received a grant of the ten tribes, his attempt now to disturb the possession of the house of David could by no means be excused, for two tribes had been expressly reserved to David when the ten were given away. The witnesses do not divide: the covenant of salt is God's own guarantee of the Davidic throne, and Abijah stakes the justice of his cause on a promise as enduring as salt is incorruptible.
6:
"Yet Jeroboam the son of Nebat, the servant of Solomon the son of David, is risen up, and hath rebelled against his lord" — Abijah now turns from the right of David's house to the wrong of Jeroboam's origin, and the witnesses fasten on the pointed word "servant." The reformed John Gill observes that Abijah names Jeroboam "the servant of Solomon" by way of great contempt, as Jarchi notes — for he had been no more than the general receiver of Solomon's tax in the tribe of Ephraim, a subordinate officer who had turned upon the house that raised him. Abijah will not dignify the northern throne by naming its holder a king, but a servant who lifted his hand against the master that had promoted him. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott anchors the charge in the earlier history, pointing to 1 Kings 11:26, where Jeroboam, Solomon's servant, first lifted up his hand against the king, and to the account of his rising and flight that follows there.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the first move in Abijah's exposure of his enemy's true weakness. Having asserted the divine right of David's line, the king now reminds the northern host that their kingdom was born in a servant's revolt against his lord, propped up by frivolous and worthless men who gathered around Jeroboam and rose against Rehoboam. The rebellion, on this reading, is not merely a political fact but a moral one: the whole northern polity rests on an act of treachery.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry develops the ingratitude at the heart of it. There was, he says, a great deal of dishonesty and disingenuousness in Jeroboam's first setting himself up: he rebelled against his lord who had preferred him, promoting him as we read at 1 Kings 11:28, and basely took advantage of Rehoboam's weakness in a critical moment when, in gratitude to his old master and in justice to his title, he ought rather to have stood by him and helped to secure the people in their allegiance. Instead he headed a party against him and made a prey of him — conduct, Henry insists, that was unworthily done and in which he could not expect to prosper. Nor does Henry pretend that Abijah's own hands were clean; but it is the cause of the covenant kingdom he pleads, and on that ground Jeroboam's crown, whatever grant of the ten tribes God had permitted, was reached by a road of rebellion and ingratitude. So the witnesses agree in the substance of the charge: it is on that crooked foundation that Abijah bids the northern army consider whether their cause can stand.
7:
The charge sharpens as Abijah describes the company Jeroboam kept — "there are gathered unto him vain men, the children of Belial" — who prevailed against Rehoboam "when he was young and tenderhearted, and could not withstand them." The reformed John Gill glosses the "vain men" as those void of the fear of God and of all that is good. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the philology: the word rendered "vain men" is the same used of the reckless followers of Abimelech and of the freebooter Jephthah at Judges 9:4 and Judges 11:3, and "children of Belial" means literally sons of worthlessness, men of low character — neither phrase, he notes, occurring again in the Chronicles.

The description of Rehoboam as "young and tenderhearted" raises a difficulty the witnesses handle in opposite ways. Ellicott renders it "a youth and soft of heart, faint-hearted," and frankly calls the expression inexact, for Rehoboam was forty-one years old at his accession according to 2 Chronicles 12:13, and in the earlier narrative he appears haughty and imperious rather than timid; Abijah, he suggests, is naturally anxious to put the case as strongly as possible against Jeroboam and to avoid all blame of his own father. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws the reverse inference from the same words: since Rehoboam is here called young and tender-hearted, he reasons, he could not really have been forty-one when he came to the throne — the description is to be trusted and the number reconsidered. Clarke also glosses "children of Belial" as men of the most abandoned principles, or men without consideration, education, or restraint.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry keeps to the moral portrait: those who supported Jeroboam are called "vain men," men that did not act from any steady principle but were given to change, and men of Belial who were for shaking off the yoke of government and setting over themselves rulers who would do just as they pleased. Ellicott presses the point further: the closing clause "could not withstand them" means rather that Rehoboam did not show himself firm — a charge of weak resolve, not of tender years, which fits a man of forty-one better than the word "young" would allow; and Clarke, for his part, treats both the king's supposed youth and the disputed army-numbers as places where the received text must be weighed against the plain sense of the narrative. The witnesses thus leave a genuine disagreement standing between Clarke, who lets the epithet correct the chronology, and Ellicott, who lets the chronology expose the epithet as Abijah's partisan overstatement — while all agree on the substance, that Jeroboam's throne was won by worthless men exploiting a moment of royal weakness.
8:
Abijah now names the presumption of the whole enterprise: "ye think to withstand the kingdom of the LORD in the hand of the sons of David; and ye be a great multitude, and there are with you golden calves." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott unfolds the logic. "Ye think" is literally "ye say" — in your hearts; "to withstand the kingdom" means to show yourselves strong before it; and "in the hand of the sons of David" means the kingdom which Jehovah holds by the instrumentality of the house of David as his earthly representatives. The northern host, he explains, trusts in two things — its numbers and its calves — believing itself assured of divine aid; but that trust is delusive, for Jeroboam himself made the very objects of their devotion, mere idols like those exposed at Isaiah 44:9, and they have superseded the only lawful worship of the LORD.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse the same way: Abijah points out the vanity of his enemies' confidence in their great multitude and their gods, at the very time when they had driven out the priests of Jahve. To imagine oneself strong against the kingdom that Jahve rules through David's sons, while leaning on golden calves, is to lean on nothing. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley puts it starkly: there is that among you which may damp your confidence, for you worship those images which God abhors.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the double edge of the taunt. Abijah does not ask the ten tribes to return to the house of David — that matter was settled by the divine determination, and he acquiesced in it — but only to desist from fighting against it and to be content with what they had. Then he shows them the ruin they carry within their own camp: "you have calves for your gods, that are unable to protect and help you, and will certainly cause the true and living God to oppose you; those will be Achans, troublers of your camp." A multitude that fights the kingdom of the LORD while trusting in idols carries the seeds of its own defeat, however great its numbers. So the witnesses converge on the irony Abijah presses: the very things the northern army counts as its strength — its host and its calves — are in truth its weakness, because the one is arrayed against God's kingdom and the other is no god at all. The reformed John Gill sums it simply: they thought to prevail over the kingdom the LORD had delivered into the hand of David's sons.
9:
The indictment reaches the worship itself: "Have ye not cast out the priests of the LORD, the sons of Aaron, and the Levites, and made you priests after the manner of the nations of other lands?" The reformed John Gill sees policy behind the sacrilege — Jeroboam expelled the lawful priests because they would not sacrifice to his idols, that they might not instruct the people in the pure worship of God, and that he and his people might be free from the payment of tithes and firstfruits. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the terms: "cast out" means banished, and "after the manner of the nations" means priests drawn from all classes of the people rather than from the divinely chosen tribe of Levi — for the surrounding heathen, unlike Israel, had no exclusive sacerdotal caste, a departure already noted at 1 Kings 12:31.

Both grammatical-historical witnesses dwell on the strange clause about consecration — "whosoever cometh to consecrate himself with a young bullock and seven rams." Keil and Delitzsch explain that "to fill the hand" is the legal idiom for investing a man with the priesthood, and that the animals named point to the consecration rite of Exodus 29:1 and following: any man who came with such sacrifices could have himself made a priest of the no-gods. Ellicott confirms that "to fill a man's hand" was the technical phrase for instituting a priest, as at Exodus 28:41, but notes that "a young bullock and seven rams" is not a full account of the Mosaic requirement — either Abijah did not care to be exact, or Jeroboam had modified the rule. The compound "no-gods," he adds, is formed like the "no-god" of Deuteronomy 32:17, the calves being spoken of as mere idols, though Jeroboam had set them up as representations of the God of Israel. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the clause as the price of office: he who could provide a young bullock and seven rams was received into that spurious and wicked priesthood.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry drives the contempt home. Jeroboam, he says, made any man a priest that had a mind to the office and would be at the charge of the consecration, though ever so much a scandal to it; and such, though very unfit to be priests, were fittest of all to be their priests — for what is more agreeable to gods that are no gods than priests that are no priests? Like to like, Henry concludes, both pretenders and usurpers. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley glosses the whole simply, that to fill the hand was to make oneself a priest. The witnesses agree: in casting out Aaron's sons Jeroboam had not merely changed the ministry but hollowed out the worship, leaving a counterfeit priesthood to serve a counterfeit god.
10:
Against the counterfeit worship of the north Abijah now sets the true worship of Judah: "But as for us, the LORD is our God, and we have not forsaken him; and the priests, which minister unto the LORD, are the sons of Aaron." The reformed John Gill hears in it the confession of a people who acknowledge no other God — not the calves at Dan and Bethel, but the one living and true God, whom the Targum calls the Word of the LORD. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the claim precisely: Judah has not forsaken the LORD "in so far as they observed the legal Jahve-worship," the sons of Aaron serving as priests and the Levites performing their appointed service.

Here the witnesses face a real difficulty, for the parallel at 1 Kings 15:3 says of Abijah himself that his heart was not perfect with the LORD his God. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott resolves it carefully: that passage is by no means incompatible with this one, for what Abijah here asserts is simply true — that Judah had maintained the Levitical priesthood and its associated worship, a work that none but Levites could rightly perform. The claim, in other words, concerns the public and official religion of the kingdom, not the private piety of the king. The wesleyan-arminian witnesses affirm the same on the plain level: Adam Clarke, that Judah had not abandoned the LORD but still served him according to his own law; John Wesley, that "the LORD" means Jehovah, the only true God, and that Judah maintained the worship the north had rejected.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry probes the paradox that Ellicott smooths. Abijah, he observes from the character given him in Kings, was not himself truly religious, yet he encouraged himself from the religion of his people — and it is common for those that deny the power of godliness to boast of the form of it. Many who have little religion themselves have yet so much sense and grace as to value it in others; and it was the cause of his kingdom, not his own soul, that Abijah was pleading, hoping that for the sake of the good men and good things that were in Judah God would appear for them. So the witnesses hold two truths together: the boast is objectively true of Judah's worship even as it sits uneasily with the private character of the man who makes it, and the God who honours right worship may grant a victory to a flawed king for the sake of a faithful people.
11:
Abijah presses the contrast into detail, enumerating the daily round of the temple: "they burn unto the LORD every morning and every evening burnt sacrifices and sweet incense; the shewbread also they set in order upon the pure table; and the candlestick of gold with the lamps thereof, to burn every evening." The reformed John Gill notes the division of labour — the priests offered the burnt sacrifice upon one altar and the incense upon another, both every day, morning and evening, so that Judah's faithfulness lay not in the grandeur of its worship but in its unbroken constancy, kept up without a gap while the north improvised. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott anchors each item in the Mosaic law: the daily sacrifice of Exodus 29:38, the incense of spices of Exodus 30:7, the pile of bread on the pure table and the golden lampstand with its lamps of Leviticus 24:5 — the faithful observance of which is what the text calls "keeping the charge of the LORD," and the neglect of which is "forsaking" him, in the language of Leviticus 8:35.

Ellicott notices a curiosity: only one table and one candlestick are mentioned here, though the description of the temple furnishings at 2 Chronicles 4:7 speaks of ten of each. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley supplies the answer — Abijah says "table" and "candlestick" in the singular though there were ten of each, because ordinarily only one of each was in use at a time for these purposes — and adds that the table was of pure gold, as at Exodus 25:23.

Yet Wesley will not let the boast pass without a probe of the heart behind it. In the words "we keep the charge of the LORD our God," he suspects, Abijah perhaps flattered himself that his keeping up of the external worship of God would make satisfaction for the errors of his life. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the same clause more generously as the true ground of Judah's confidence: both the temple service and the temple furniture were of God's own appointing, and his appointment they abide by, neither adding nor diminishing — a plea that the very things they defend in the field are the things God has prescribed. The witnesses thus let the verse do double duty — a precise inventory of the lawful worship that distinguished Judah from Israel, and, in Wesley's hand, a quiet warning that the outward form, however exactly kept, is no substitute for a heart right with the God it professes to serve.
12:
The speech ends where it was always tending — in God himself: "behold, God himself is with us for our captain, and his priests with sounding trumpets to cry alarm against you. O children of Israel, fight ye not against the LORD God of your fathers; for ye shall not prosper." The reformed John Gill hears the plain claim that God is present to go before Judah's armies and fight their battles. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw the conclusion Abijah intends: at the head of Judah stand not two golden calves but the true God and his priests, with the alarm-trumpets appointed to sound — the very trumpets that Numbers 10:9 made the pledge that God would remember his people in war and deliver them from their enemies. Charles Ellicott confirms the reading, "with us at the head are the God and his priests, and the trumpets of alarm to sound alarm against you," pointing to the same law and to the priestly trumpeters of Numbers 31:6. The trumpet, on this reading, is not merely an instrument of alarm but a token of covenant memory, sounded to remind God of his people and his people of their God.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses sound the warning that closes the speech. Adam Clarke sets the contrast bluntly — "ye have golden calves; we have the living and omnipotent Jehovah" — and reads the trumpets as a threat: when the priests sound them, it will be the token that the vengeance of the LORD is about to descend. John Wesley presses the double folly of the northern cause: to fight against the God of almighty power is folly, but to fight against the God of their own fathers, the God their ancestors served, is treachery and base ingratitude besides — and against such an adversary they cannot prosper.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the note of holy confidence. God is Judah's captain, and as a token of his presence they have his priests sounding the trumpets according to the law, both a martial and a sacred sound, an assurance that in the day of battle they shall be remembered before the LORD and saved from their enemies. Nothing, Henry observes, is more effectual to embolden men and put spirit into them than to be sure that God is with them and fights for them. And so Abijah's appeal ends not in a boast of arms but in a summons and a warning — that to array a great host against the covenant kingdom of the LORD is to be sure of ruin, for the God of their fathers cannot be beaten by the children who have deserted him.
13:
With the speech unanswered, the scene turns from words to stratagem: "Jeroboam caused an ambushment to come about behind them; so they were before Judah, and the ambushment behind them." The reformed John Gill pictures it happening even as Abijah spoke — while the king of Judah was making his oration, Jeroboam quietly detached a party from his army to circle round and lie in wait behind Abijah's host. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay out the manoeuvre: Jeroboam sent the troops appointed for ambush to come upon Judah's rear, so that his main body stood before them and the ambush behind, and the men of Judah, when they turned to fight, saw war both before and behind — a desperate position from which they cried to the LORD while the priests blew the trumpets. The plan was shrewd, and to human eyes the trap was already sprung: the men of Judah now stood with an enemy on either hand, the main body of Israel before them and the hidden detachment closing from behind.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the ambush as the measure of the man. Jeroboam offered no answer at all to Abijah's speech; though it was much to the purpose, he resolved not to heed it, for he came to fight, not to dispute, thinking the longest sword, and not the better cause, would determine the matter. He felt, Henry supposes, a sovereign contempt for the harangue — "one stratagem is worth twenty such speeches" — and while a parley was in progress he basely took the advantage of it, laying his ambush behind Judah against all the laws of arms. What honour, Henry asks, could be expected in a servant when he came to reign? Abijah was for peace, but when he spoke, they were for war, in the very case of the psalmist at Psalms 120:7.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses read the peril and the providence together. Adam Clarke observes that while Abijah was reproving them Jeroboam divided his army and sent a part to take him in the rear — a stroke that must have proved fatal to Judah had not the LORD interposed. John Wesley likewise notes that Jeroboam took the advantage of the discourse to lay his ambush, making no answer to all that Abijah had said, for the longest sword he thought would determine the matter, not the better cause. The witnesses agree in the irony: the man who would not meet an argument with an argument met it with a trap, and so brought Judah into the very extremity in which the power of God would be most plainly seen.
14:
Caught between two fronts, Judah turns to the only quarter that is never closed: "when Judah looked back, behold, the battle was before and behind; and they cried unto the LORD, and the priests sounded with the trumpets." The reformed John Gill notes simply that they looked back on hearing the noise of the ambush behind them. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott compares the tactic to the ambuscade by which Ai was taken at Joshua 8:20 and the snare laid at Gibeah, and corrects a misreading — "Judah looked back" does not mean they were caught unprepared, but that they perceived, on turning, the war set against them on either hand; the priests, he adds, "were sounding," the trumpets already blowing. Keil and Delitzsch carry the action forward: in this dangerous position the men of Judah cried to the LORD, the priests blew the trumpets, and when they raised the war-cry God smote their enemies so that they took to flight.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the verse a small treatise on prayer under extremity. Judah was brought into a great strait and a great fright, the battle before and behind; and a good cause, he observes, one even designed to be victorious, may for a season be involved in embarrassment and distress — it was David's own case, compassed about like bees at Psalms 118:10. But in their distress, when danger was on every side, which way should they look but upward? It is an unspeakable comfort, Henry says, that no enemy, however powerful or politic, and no stratagem or ambushment, can cut off our communication with heaven; our way thither is always open. So they cried unto the LORD; and though we may hope they had prayed before they engaged, the distress they were in made them renew their prayers and quickened them to be importunate — for God brings his people into straits that he may teach them to cry unto him, and earnest praying is crying.

Henry reads the priests' trumpets as more than a martial signal: it was a sacred sound as well, giving the soldiers assurance of God's presence and putting life into their faith. The witnesses converge on the turning-point of the battle — that the decisive act was not a movement of troops but a cry to heaven, answered by the God who had promised, through the trumpets, to remember his people in the day of war. That the historian pauses, in the very crisis of the ambush, to record the prayer and the trumpet rather than any movement of troops is itself the lesson: Judah's first weapon was not the sword but the appeal to the God of their fathers.
15:
"Then the men of Judah gave a shout; and as the men of Judah shouted, it came to pass that God smote Jeroboam and all Israel before Abijah and Judah." The reformed John Gill takes the shout for a soldiers' cry — Judah taking heart at the sound of the trumpets, both to encourage one another and to intimidate the enemy. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the moment in sharp sequence: the verb here rendered "gave a shout" is the same used for sounding the alarm with the trumpets a few verses before, but here it rightly means the war-cry of the warriors, raised immediately after the priests had blown their blast, as Gideon's men shouted at Judges 7:18. And the words "God smote Jeroboam and all Israel" describe, he says, the wild panic that seized the northern host when they heard the shout of their foes — the same phrase used of the rout at Judges 20:35 and again by the Chronicler at 2 Chronicles 14:12.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley gathers the theology of the cry. It is an unspeakable comfort, he says, that no stratagem or ambush can cut off the believer's communication with heaven; to the cry of prayer Judah added the shout of faith, and so became more than conquerors. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply record the result — as the men of Judah raised their shout, God smote Jeroboam and all Israel, and the great host began to break. The brevity of the notice is itself striking — there is no account of Judah's tactics, no feat of arms singled out, only the shout and then the bare sentence that God smote; the historian will allow no room to suppose the day was won by anything but the LORD.

The decisive word in the verse, all the witnesses agree, is "God." It was not the shout that won the day, nor the trumpets, nor any superiority of Judah's arms, but the LORD who used the shout as the occasion of his own stroke. The men of Judah cried and shouted; God smote. The panic that unstrung the eight hundred thousand came from heaven, so that the very moment of Judah's greatest danger — hemmed in before and behind — became the moment of Israel's collapse. The witnesses read it as the visible answer to the prayer of the previous verse: the promise attached to the priestly trumpets, that God would remember his people in war, is here made good in a single sentence, and the battle is decided not by the longer sword but by the God whom Jeroboam had presumed to fight.
16:
"And the children of Israel fled before Judah; and God delivered them into their hand." The reformed John Gill describes the collapse: the northern army was thrown into such fright and consternation that they could not stand their ground or engage at all, but took to flight at once, their vast numbers now only a hindrance to their own retreat. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the flight as the immediate fruit of the divine stroke of the previous verse — Israel fled before Judah, and God gave them into their hand, so that Abijah and his people were able to inflict a great defeat, in which more than half of Jeroboam's whole army would fall.

The heart of the verse, the witnesses agree, lies in its second clause, and in the subject of the sentence. It is not said merely that Judah was strong, or that Israel was weak, but that "God delivered them into their hand" — the victory is credited, at the very moment of its winning, to the LORD and not to the arm of Judah. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, tracing the rout, dwells on this divine agency: God smote Jeroboam and his army with such terror and amazement that they could not strike a stroke, but fled with the greatest precipitation imaginable, so that the battle was the LORD's, who would thus chastise the idolatry of Israel and own the house of David.

Yet Henry will not let the triumph be read without grief. It was, he laments, the sad effect of division — the blood of Israelites shed like water by Israelites, while the heathen neighbours, to whom the name of Israel had once been a terror, looked on and cried, "Aha! so would we have it." The deliverance of Judah was at the same time the slaughter of the covenant nation's own sons, a victory that even the victors might mourn. So the witnesses hold the verse in its double light: the flight of Israel is God's own act of judgment upon the calf-worship, a handing-over of the guilty into the power of the faithful; and it is also a tragedy within the family of Jacob, in which the true God vindicates David's throne only by the ruin of a host of his own people who had followed a servant's rebellion into the field. The agency throughout is God's — he delivered them — and the lesson is that a nation which trusts in numbers and idols is given over, in the day of battle, into the hand of those who trust in him.
17:
The scale of the defeat is now told: "Abijah and his people slew them with a great slaughter; so there fell down slain of Israel five hundred thousand chosen men." The reformed John Gill notes only that they slew them as they fled, pursuing — the great slaughter falling upon a host already broken and in flight, not upon an army standing to fight. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch reckon the fallen at more than half of Jeroboam's entire army, and Charles Ellicott renders the Hebrew vividly — "they smote in them a great smiting," in the idiom of Numbers 11:33 — insisting, against the Speaker's Commentary, that the narrative plainly describes the issue of a single great and decisive encounter, not a slaughter spread over many days.

Here the debate opened at v.3 returns in force. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke again finds the figure incredible if pressed and asks whether we should not read fifty thousand, pointing back to his long argument that the numbers of the chapter are overcharged. Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch defend the text while conceding the difficulty: the result is indeed incredible if the numbers are taken with strict literalness, but it seems more reasonable, in Keil's words, to see in them "only a numerical expression of the belief of contemporaries of the war" — that both kings had levied all their fighting men, and that Jeroboam was defeated with the loss of more than half his host. Ellicott notes that the Syriac reads five thousand, and dismisses as absurd the suggestion of Reuss that Abijah's four hundred thousand stood "still intact" after the battle, so that one might ask why they did not press on to reduce the northern kingdom. The point of the figure, for the grammatical-historical witnesses, is not statistical precision but the completeness of the overthrow: whatever the exact tally, Jeroboam's field-army had ceased to exist.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry accepts the received number and reads it as a wonder of judgment: they put to the sword five hundred thousand chosen men, more, it is said, than were ever killed in any one battle in all history — but the battle was the LORD's, who would thus chastise the idolatry of Israel and own the house of David. So the verse leaves the two schools where v.3 had left them: Clarke and the grammatical-historical witnesses genuinely at odds over whether the figure is literal or a copyist's or contemporary's swelling of it, yet agreed on the thing signified — that the northern army was broken beyond recovery, and that so vast a ruin could be ascribed to nothing but the hand of God against a kingdom that had forsaken him.
18:
The verse states the outcome and, more importantly, its cause: "the children of Israel were brought under at that time, and the children of Judah prevailed, because they relied upon the LORD God of their fathers." The reformed John Gill marks the limit of the victory — Israel was humbled and weakened, yet not reduced again to the government of the house of David. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in the vocabulary: "brought under" means humbled or bowed down, the same word used of Rehoboam's humbling at 2 Chronicles 12:6; "prevailed" means grew strong; and "relied upon" is the verb of leaning or staying oneself upon God, as at Isaiah 10:20. Keil and Delitzsch read the two clauses as cause and effect — the men of Judah became mighty precisely because they relied upon Jahve their God. That reliance, they stress, is the sole reason the text offers; it does not appeal to Judah's courage or generalship, but grounds the whole outcome in the disposition of the heart toward God.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on that reliance, citing the Targum's gloss that Judah prevailed "because they depended on the Word of the God of their fathers." It is the one explanation the text offers for a victory otherwise inexplicable, and the witnesses agree it is the theological hinge of the whole chapter. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws it out most fully: in their distress Judah relied on the God of their fathers, depended upon his power to help them, and committed themselves to him — and the prayer of faith is the prevailing prayer, this being the very thing by which we overcome the world, even our faith, in the language of 1 John 5:4. To the cry of prayer they had added the shout of faith, and so became more than conquerors.

Henry also notes the providence in the limit Gill observed: the children of Israel, though not brought back to the house of David, were for that time brought under — and had the blow not been so restrained, they would surely have been reduced, but the determinate counsel of God had ordered otherwise. So the witnesses converge on the sentence's own logic. The disproportion of numbers, the ambush, the double front, all counted for nothing; what turned the day was that Judah leaned on the LORD, and a people that leans on him is made strong, while a people that trusts in calves and multitudes is bowed down. The verse gives the reader the moral of the battle in a single clause, and the witnesses are unanimous that it is the true one.
19:
Victory becomes conquest: "Abijah pursued after Jeroboam, and took cities from him — Bethel with the towns thereof, and Jeshanah with the towns thereof, and Ephrain with the towns thereof." The reformed John Gill notes simply that Abijah pursued as Jeroboam fled. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify what they can: Bethel is the modern Beitin, near the Ai of Joshua 7:2; Jeshanah occurs only here and its position has not been ascertained; and the third city, written Ephron in the text and Ephrain in the margin, cannot be the Mount Ephron of Joshua 15:9, which lay far to the south near Jerusalem, but must be sought farther north near Bethel, perhaps to be identified with Ophrah of Benjamin.

Charles Ellicott supplies the historical sequel and refines the geography. The three cities and their districts, he observes, were only temporarily annexed to Judah; for according to 1 Kings 15:17 Baasha of Israel in the next reign attempted to fortify Ramah, only some five miles north of Jerusalem, and must first have recovered these towns. Bethel he too locates at Beitin, citing Genesis 12:8; Jeshanah he leaves unidentified, though he connects it with the Isanas of Josephus; and for the third city he weighs Ophrah near Bethel, mentioned at Judges 6:11, and the town of Ephraim near the wilderness at John 11:54, suggesting that Ophrah and Ephraim may be one and the same place. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds only the Targum's rendering of Bethel as "Bethlehem" and confesses of Jeshanah and its neighbours, "we know not where these towns lay" — content, as often, to mark the limits of what can be known.

The witnesses do not divide over the meaning of the conquest, only over the map. What matters, they agree, is that the rout of the great army was followed by a real and measurable gain — the taking of Bethel above all, the very seat of one of Jeroboam's golden calves, from the king who had trusted in it. That the cities were held only for a time, and would be lost again in the next reign, does not diminish the point the Chronicler presses: God, having smitten the idolatrous host, gave into Abijah's hand the strongholds of the calf-worship itself, so that the victory of faith reached even to the shrines where the rebellion had begun. Yet Ellicott's cross-reference to Baasha's later fortifying of Ramah keeps the gain in proportion: the towns were a real conquest, but the frontier would shift again within a reign, the prizes of one king passing back to the sons of the next.
20:
"Neither did Jeroboam recover strength again in the days of Abijah; and the LORD struck him, and he died." The reformed John Gill reads the first clause of military power — Jeroboam could no longer bring an army into the field to fight Abijah, his power, once double Judah's, broken past mending. But the second clause raises two questions the witnesses cannot fully settle. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke raises the first bluntly: who died — Abijah or Jeroboam? Rabbins and Christians, he says, are divided, and the point is not yet settled; but the prevailing opinion, which he shares, is that Jeroboam is meant, struck at this time with the disease of which he died about two years later, for he did not die until two years after Abijah, as the dates of 1 Kings 14:20 and 1 Kings 15:9 require.

The second question is what the blow was. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confess the remark is not clear, since we know nothing further of the end of Jeroboam's life; it can hardly refer to the defeat just narrated, which he outlived by several years, and they are more inclined to understand it of the stroke of 1 Kings 14:1, when God announced by Ahijah the extermination of his house and took away his son. Charles Ellicott resists that identification. All that is known of Jeroboam's death, he grants, is that it fell two years after Abijah's; but the phrase cannot mean merely that God "visited him with misfortune till his death," as Zockler proposed. Rather, Jeroboam's death is regarded as a judicial visitation, in the same idiom used of the LORD's smiting of Nabal at 1 Samuel 25:38 — the verse states that Jeroboam remained powerless to injure his neighbour through the rest of Abijah's reign, and that the circumstances of his death were such that men recognized in them the finger of God. Both grammatical-historical witnesses thus agree that the death was a stroke of judgment, and differ only over which stroke Scripture intends.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the plain moral: Jeroboam had escaped the sword of Abijah, but God struck him — and there is no escaping his sword. So the witnesses leave the verse with its particulars in dispute, whether the blow was the prophecy against his house or a final judicial stroke, and even whose death is recorded here — yet united in the sense that Jeroboam's end was no accident of nature but the hand of the God he had defied, overtaking at last the servant who had risen against his lord.
21:
After the enemy's decline comes the victor's flourishing: "Abijah waxed mighty, and married fourteen wives, and begat twenty and two sons, and sixteen daughters." The reformed John Gill takes "waxed mighty" broadly — Abijah increased in riches and numbers, in power and authority, and in his family — the word for "waxed mighty" marking a real accession of strength, not a bare continuance of it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the notice as the counterpart to Jeroboam's decline: while his rival could not recover, Abijah established himself in his kingdom and took to himself fourteen wives. Since he reigned only three years, they observe, he must have had the greater number of these wives and children already before his accession, as may be gathered from the account of his father's house at 2 Chronicles 11:21; the growth of his family stands here as a sign of his increasing strength.

Charles Ellicott reads the verse the same way and sharpens the contrast. A full stop, he suggests, should be placed after the first clause: "Abijah strengthened himself." Then the two facts of his many wives and his many children are simply set side by side — but with a tacit contrast suggested between the number of Abijah's offspring and the speedy extirpation of the house of Jeroboam, whose dynasty would soon be cut off root and branch. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds a political motive, supposing that Abijah made alliances with the neighbouring powers by taking their daughters to wife. Keil and Delitzsch, however, caution that the marriages are not to be dated after his establishment: a reign of but three years left no room to gather so large a household in its aftermath, so that the many wives and children are the sign, not the reward, of a strength Abijah already possessed.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, who counts the children as thirty-eight in all, at first hears the language of blessing — "happy is the man that hath his quiver full of those arrows." But he will not end on that note. For all his triumphs, Henry observes, the number of Abijah's months was cut off in the midst, and soon after his victories death conquered the conqueror; perhaps he was too much lifted up with his success, and therefore God would not let him live long to enjoy the honour of it. So the witnesses read the verse in two registers at once: as the outward token of a strength that God had granted in contrast to his fallen enemy, and, with Henry, as a flourishing that was not to last — a full house and a mighty name in a reign that would close, almost as soon as it had risen, in an early grave.
22:
The chapter closes with the familiar formula of the annals: "the rest of the acts of Abijah, and his ways, and his sayings, are written in the story of the prophet Iddo." The reformed John Gill lingers on that last word, "sayings" — the record preserved not only Abijah's warlike actions and his course of life but some remarkable sayings of his, for he was a man of wisdom and eloquence, as the speech of this very chapter shows — so that the sacred writer, in pointing to the fuller record, pays a passing tribute to a king remembered as much for his words as for his war. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the term rendered "story": the Hebrew is midrash, "commentary," and this, he believes, is the first place in Scripture where a midrash or commentary is mentioned. The commentary of the prophet Iddo is now lost, and what his sayings were we cannot tell; but from the specimen preserved in this chapter, Clarke judges, Abijah appears to have been a very able speaker, who knew well how to make the best use of his argument.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes the artistry of the Hebrew phrase, an alliteration in "his ways and his sayings," and confirms that "story" renders midrash, the source to which the Chronicler here refers his readers. Keil and Delitzsch likewise identify the midrash of the prophet Iddo as the writer's authority, and observe that the rest which the next notice records, at 2 Chronicles 14:1, was itself a result of Abijah's great victory over Jeroboam — the quiet that followed the battle being the last fruit of the day on Mount Zemaraim.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in the mention of Abijah's ways and sayings the sign of a man who had a manner and a wisdom peculiarly his own, recorded with his acts in the history of those times — the eloquence that had thundered from Mount Zemaraim now folded away into a single clause. Yet Henry sets beside it the sober fact that the number of Abijah's months was soon cut off; the eloquent king who had reasoned so forcibly from the mountain, and conquered so completely in the valley, is dismissed in a single verse to the lost book of a prophet, his reign of three years over almost before it is told. So the witnesses close the chapter as the Chronicler does — with a pointer to a record now vanished, a last tribute to the eloquence of Abijah, and the reminder that even a great victory and a mighty house pass quickly into the brief formula with which Scripture lays a king to rest.
